name: Marie Mason
description: Earth First! organizer, IWW member, and mother of two convicted of involvement with a December 1999 arson at a Michigan State University genetics laboratory at Agriculture Hall and a January 2000 arson of logging equipment in Mesick, Michigan, both claimed by the Earth Liberation Front. Arrested March 10, 2008 by federal agents in Cincinnati. Received the longest ELF-related sentence of any defendant.

cases.charges: Arson, conspiracy (Earth Liberation Front actions)

name: Matthew DePalma
description: Anarchist convicted of illegally possessing Molotov cocktails allegedly intended to be used at the 2008 Republican National Convention in St. Paul. Arrested on August 30, 2008 by FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force agents at a Minneapolis residence after being surveilled by a paid informant who met him at a CrimethInc Convergence near Waldo, Wisconsin. Pleaded guilty October 21, 2008 to one count of possession of a destructive device.

cases.charges: Possession of a destructive device (Molotov cocktails)

name: Erik Oseland
description: One of the RNC 8 organizers arrested August 30, 2008 by Ramsey County sheriff's deputies, with help from a paid federal informant, in connection with protests against the 2008 Republican National Convention in St. Paul. Charged with Conspiracy to Riot in Furtherance of Terrorism under the Minnesota PATRIOT Act — the first time the state's terrorism statute was used against political organizers.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to Riot in Furtherance of Terrorism (MN PATRIOT Act)

name: David Japenga
description: Convicted of four misdemeanors and a felony for breaking windows at Citizens Bank, Irish Design Center, and Quizno's on September 24, 2009 during the G-20 summit protests in Pittsburgh. Held at the Allegheny County Jail in Pittsburgh.

cases.charges: Four misdemeanors and one felony (property destruction during G-20 protests)

name: David Guy McKay
description: Arrested in September 2008 at age 22 at the Republican National Convention in St. Paul for allegedly possessing incendiary devices. Defenders argue he was entrapped by FBI informant Brandon Darby, an older and more experienced activist-provocateur. McKay is one of the Texas 2, alongside Bradley Crowder.

cases.charges: Possession of incendiary devices (Molotov cocktails)

name: Luis V. Rodriguez
description: Apache/Chicano indigenous rights activist convicted in California of the murder of two police officers, a case supporters say was a frame-up tied to his organizing. Held at Kern Valley State Prison.

cases.charges: Murder of two police officers

name: Abdul Majid
description: Queens-born Black Panther Party and Republic of New Afrika organizer who worked in the Grass Roots Advisory Council anti-poverty program in the 1960s and ran free health clinic and free breakfast programs in the BPP. Tried three times with co-defendant Bashir Hameed for the murder of a New York police officer — first trial hung along racial lines, second declared a mistrial, third resulted in conviction. Sentenced to 33 years to life. One of the NY-3 / Queens 2.

cases.charges: Murder and attempted murder of NYC police officer
cases.convicted: Yes — convicted at third trial after a hung jury and a mistrial

name: Maliki Shakur Latine
description: Bronx-born Black Panther who first organized with the Nation of Islam before joining the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense with his brother Shaqwan in 1969. Briefly held at Riker's Island where he met Lumumba Shakur of the Panther 21, Latine later went underground following relentless COINTELPRO attacks and joined the Black Liberation Army. Captured after six years underground and held in New York state prison.

cases.charges: Counts arising from BLA activity

name: Marshall Eddie Conway
description: Minister of Defense of the Baltimore chapter of the Black Panther Party in 1970 who also worked at the U.S. Postal Service. Some Baltimore chapter founders were undercover Baltimore Police officers reporting daily on his activities, while the FBI maintained simultaneous surveillance. After an April 23, 1970 police shooting Conway was identified by Officer Nolan from a photograph and arrested at the Post Office the next day, charged with the homicide and attempted homicide of officers.

cases.charges: Homicide and attempted homicide of Baltimore police officers

name: Sekou Kambui
description: New Afrikan political prisoner born in Gadsden, Alabama who organized SCLC, CORE, and SNCC security in the 1960s civil rights movement before affiliating with the Black Panther Party in 1967 and joining the Republic of New Afrika. Coordinated activity with the Alabama Black Liberation Front and the Inmates for Action Defense Committee in the mid-1970s and served as a BLA soldier. Captured January 2, 1975 during a traffic stop in north Birmingham; serving two consecutive life sentences for the alleged 1975 murders of two white men in Alabama.

cases.charges: Two counts of murder (Alabama, 1975)

name: Zolo Azania
description: Former Black Panther convicted in connection with a 1981 bank robbery in Gary, Indiana that left a police officer dead. Arrested miles from the scene while walking unarmed; co-defendants received 60-year sentences while Azania was given the death penalty after the prosecution used his political history to influence sentencing. Supporters say witnesses were intimidated, favorable evidence suppressed, and false expert testimony presented.

cases.charges: Bank robbery, murder of Gary, Indiana police officer

name: Herman Wallace
description: Member of the Angola 3, a group of Black liberationists who organized a chapter of the Black Panther Party inside Louisiana State Penitentiary in 1971 and led prisoners in strikes and sit-ins to desegregate the prison, end systematic rape and violence, and improve conditions. Convicted with Albert Woodfox of the 1972 stabbing murder of 23-year-old prison guard Brent Miller and held in solitary confinement at Elayn Hunt Correctional Center. Released October 2013 after his conviction was vacated; died days later.

cases.charges: Murder of prison guard Brent Miller (1972)

name: Albert Woodfox
description: Member of the Angola 3 who helped found a Black Panther Party chapter inside Louisiana State Penitentiary in 1971, organizing jailhouse legal work, hunger strikes, and protests against systematic abuse. Convicted with Herman Wallace of the 1972 stabbing murder of guard Brent Miller and held in solitary confinement at David Wade Correctional Center — among the longest-held solitary confinement prisoners in U.S. history. Released February 19, 2016 after 43 years in solitary; died of COVID-19 in 2022.

cases.charges: Murder of prison guard Brent Miller (1972)

name: Ed Poindexter
description: Member of the Omaha chapter of the Black Panther Party and one of the Nebraska 2, convicted of the August 17, 1970 dynamite-suitcase killing of Omaha Police Officer Larry Minard. The Omaha Police withheld exculpatory evidence at trial and the men were targets of the FBI's COINTELPRO. Amnesty International USA has recognized Poindexter as a political prisoner; Nebraska's parole board has recommended his release but political leaders have not acted.

cases.charges: Murder of Omaha Police Officer Larry Minard (1970 bombing)

name: Mondo we Langa
description: Omaha Black Panther Party member, poet, and one of the Nebraska 2 (Omaha Two), convicted of the August 17, 1970 dynamite-suitcase killing of Omaha Police Officer Larry Minard. The Omaha Police withheld exculpatory evidence at trial and the case was driven by FBI COINTELPRO operations against the Omaha Black Panthers. Amnesty International USA has recognized him as a political prisoner. Died in custody March 11, 2016.

cases.charges: Murder of Omaha Police Officer Larry Minard (1970 bombing)

name: Ruchell Magee
description: Among the longest-held political prisoners in the United States, having been locked up since 1963. Politicized in prison, Magee participated in the August 7, 1970 Marin County Courthouse rebellion — Jonathan Jackson's attempted armed liberation of political prisoners — and was the sole prisoner survivor. He has worked tirelessly as a jailhouse lawyer on his own case and on behalf of many other prisoners. Granted compassionate release July 2023; died three months later.

cases.charges: Aggravated kidnapping (Marin County Courthouse rebellion, 1970)

name: Gary Tyler
description: Sixteen-year-old Black student arrested October 7, 1974 after buses carrying Black students leaving Destrehan High School in St. Charles Parish, Louisiana were attacked by a white mob of 100 to 200 people throwing stones and bottles. After complaining about police harassment of other Black students Tyler was placed in a police car; a thorough search of the bus turned up no weapon, but days later police claimed to find a .45 caliber pistol in his seat. Convicted and held at Louisiana State Penitentiary at Angola. Released April 2016 after 41 years.

cases.charges: Murder (Destrehan school bus incident, 1974)

name: Romaine Fitzgerald
description: Compton-born Black Panther who joined the Southern California Chapter of the BPP in early 1969 as a teenager after release from the California Youth Authority. Arrested in September 1969 in connection with a police shoot-out and tried for assault on police and the murder of a security guard. One of the longest-held former Black Panther political prisoners; originally sentenced to death.

cases.charges: Assault on police, murder of security guard

name: Eric McDavid
description: Arrested January 13, 2006 along with Zachary Jenson and Lauren Weiner as part of the federal government's Green Scare campaign. Charged with conspiracy to damage and destroy property by fire and explosive after an 18-month entrapment operation by FBI informant Anna, who was paid over $65,000 to draw McDavid in and help fabricate a crime. No action was ever carried out; convicted after his co-defendants cooperated and turned state's witnesses. Conviction vacated and released January 2015 after government withheld FBI documents.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to damage and destroy property by fire and explosive

name: Jonathan Paul
description: Animal rights activist who pled guilty and was sentenced to 51 months for involvement in a 1997 Animal Liberation Front direct action in Oregon. The action — an arson at the Cavel West horse slaughterhouse in Redmond, Oregon — is credited with directly ending horse slaughtering in the United States. Prosecuted under Operation Backfire.

cases.charges: Arson, conspiracy (Animal Liberation Front)

name: Rodney Coronado
description: Pascua Yaqui Native American eco-anarchist and animal rights activist, longtime advocate for the Animal Liberation Front and spokesperson for the Earth Liberation Front. On September 16, 2010 the federal government returned him to federal prison for accepting a Facebook friend request — held to violate the terms of probation stemming from prior ALF/ELF convictions, after a year and a half on the outside.

cases.charges: Probation violation (accepting Facebook friend request)

name: Helen Woodson
description: Catholic radical originally sentenced to 27 years for robbing a bank and burning the money while reading a statement denouncing greed, capitalism, and environmental destruction, and for a 1984 Plowshares action in which she and three others used a jackhammer to chip the concrete cover of a nuclear missile silo near Whiteman Air Force Base near Knob Noster, Missouri. Sentenced again in 2006 to 51 months for threatening federal officials and pouring red paint and cranberry juice on a federal courthouse security station.

cases.charges: Threatening federal officials, property destruction at federal courthouse

name: Joyanna Zacher
description: Earth Liberation Front activist who pled guilty and was sentenced to seven years and eight months for involvement in two ELF direct actions in Oregon in 2001. One of only four Operation Backfire targets who refused to cooperate with the federal government. Held at FCI Dublin in California.

cases.charges: Arson, conspiracy (Earth Liberation Front, Oregon 2001)

name: Nathan Block
description: Earth Liberation Front activist who pled guilty and was sentenced to seven years and eight months for involvement in two ELF direct actions in Oregon in 2001. Prosecuted as part of Operation Backfire and held at FCI Lompoc.

cases.charges: Arson, conspiracy (Earth Liberation Front, Oregon 2001)

name: Michael Sykes
description: Eighteen-year-old anarchist from Lambertville, Michigan serving a 4-10 year prison sentence with $200,000-$400,000 in restitution for eco-related acts of property destruction committed when he was 17 — tried as an adult. Convicted of setting fire to two homes under construction; accused of additional construction and Kroger's semi-trailer arsons. Stated motive was opposition to suburban sprawl: he was 'tired of seeing all the forest being destroyed.'

cases.charges: Arson (anti-sprawl property destruction)

name: Grant Barnes
description: Earth Liberation activist who pled guilty and was sentenced to 12 years in prison for setting several SUVs on fire in the Denver, Colorado area. Held at Arrowhead Correctional Facility in Cañon City.

cases.charges: Arson (SUVs, Denver area)

name: Lynne Stewart
description: Veteran New York criminal defense attorney sentenced to 28 months in the Southern District of New York for providing material support to the Egyptian group al-Gama'a al-Islamiyya — a charge stemming from her representation of Sheikh Omar Abdel-Rahman, the 'blind sheikh' convicted in 1995 of conspiracy to bomb New York City landmarks. Her sentence was later increased to 10 years on appeal. Released on compassionate release December 2013 with terminal cancer; died March 2017.

cases.charges: Providing material support to a designated terrorist organization, conspiracy to defraud the United States

name: Tsutomu Shirosaki
description: Japanese national imprisoned in the United States, accused of being a member of the Japanese Red Army and of participating in a mortar attack on the U.S. embassy in Jakarta. Earlier, in 1971, he was arrested in Tokyo and sentenced to ten years for an attack on a Bank of Yokohama branch — funds said to support Japanese radical groups. Serving 30 years in U.S. federal prison; held at FCI Terre Haute Communication Management Unit.

cases.charges: Attack on U.S. embassy (Jakarta mortar attack)

name: William Gilday
description: Sixties radical and former minor league baseball player from Amesbury, Massachusetts. After a prior robbery sentence he met Stanley Bond (a Vietnam helicopter pilot) and Robert Valeri in prison; the three later enrolled at Boston-area universities through the Student Tutor Education Program and joined SDS and the Weather Underground. On September 23, 1970, Gilday and four comrades — including Bond, Valeri, Susan Saxe, and Katherine Power — robbed the State Street Bank and Trust Company in Boston of $26,585 to finance the anti-war movement; a Boston police officer was killed during the action. Sentenced to death; held at MCI Shirley. Died in custody 2011.

cases.charges: Bank robbery, murder of Boston police officer (State Street Bank, September 23, 1970)
cases.sentence: Death (commuted to life; died in custody)

name: Avelino González Claudio
description: Puerto Rican independence activist accused in August 1985 of planning and authorizing the September 12, 1983 Macheteros expropriation of $7,117,000 from a Wells Fargo armored truck in Hartford, Connecticut. The operation was carried out by the clandestine Ejército Popular Boricua – Macheteros fighting for Puerto Rican independence. Not arrested at the time, González Claudio was finally captured more than 20 years later, on February 7, 2008, in Manatí, Puerto Rico.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, armed robbery (Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation, 1983)

name: Patrice Lumumba Ford
description: Accused of membership in the so-called Portland Seven, members of which allegedly tried to travel to Afghanistan shortly after September 11, 2001 to fight alongside the Taliban against U.S. and allied forces. Refused to cooperate with the federal government and was sentenced to 18 years in prison after pleading guilty to seditious conspiracy and levying war against the United States — avoiding a potential life sentence.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, levying war against the United States

name: Rafil Dhafir
description: American Iraqi-born physician sentenced October 28, 2005 to 22 years in federal prison for violating U.S. sanctions on Iraq by sending money to Iraq through his charity Help the Needy, along with fraud, money laundering, tax evasion, and other nonviolent charges. Believed to be the only U.S. citizen imprisoned for violating the Iraq sanctions. Five other people, including his wife, pleaded guilty in connection with the case.

cases.charges: Violating Iraqi sanctions, fraud, money laundering, tax evasion

name: Patreese Johnson
description: One of the New Jersey 4, a group of young African-American lesbian women from Newark who were attacked by a male bystander while visiting Manhattan's West Village on August 18, 2006. The man made homophobic and sexist comments and told one woman he would 'f—k her straight'; when the women defended themselves a physical altercation ensued and they were arrested. Three co-defendants took plea bargains; four were convicted in April 2007. Johnson, only 19 at sentencing, received an 11-year sentence.

cases.charges: Gang assault, attempted assault (self-defense against street harasser)

name: Kevin Kjonaas
description: Animal rights organizer convicted as part of the SHAC 7 — leaders of the U.S. campaign of Stop Huntingdon Animal Cruelty against the Huntingdon Life Sciences testing laboratory. Convicted of conspiracy to violate the Animal Enterprise Protection Act, conspiracy to stalk, three counts of interstate stalking, and conspiracy to harass using a telecommunications device. Began a six-year federal sentence on November 16, 2006.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to violate the Animal Enterprise Protection Act, conspiracy to stalk, interstate stalking, conspiracy to harass via telecommunications

name: Christopher John Monfort
description: Accused of waging a one-man war against the Seattle police in the fall of 2009, including the firebombing of police vehicles and the murder of a Seattle police officer. Faced the death penalty in Washington state. Has been outspoken in court proceedings about the role of police and has called on others in the United States to confront police terror in their communities and use armed tactics if necessary. Died in custody 2017.

cases.charges: Aggravated first-degree murder of Seattle police officer, arson of police vehicles

name: Gerardo Hernández
description: One of the Cuban Five, Cuban intelligence officers who infiltrated Miami-based anti-Cuba paramilitary groups (including Comandos F4 and Brothers to the Rescue) to gather evidence of planned terrorist attacks on Cuba. Wrongly convicted in U.S. federal court in Miami on June 8, 2001 of espionage conspiracy and related charges. Hernández received two life sentences plus 15 years and was held at USP Victorville. Released December 17, 2014 as part of the U.S.-Cuba prisoner exchange.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage, conspiracy to commit murder (Brothers to the Rescue shootdown)
